coredata.dc:title: Mild Alkaline-Enhanced depolymerization of Long-Flame coal for the synthesis of Coal-Derived fluorescent carbon dots with application as probes
coredata.dc:creator: Chong, Junkai
Hu, Guangchao
Xiao, Longheng
Guo, Min
Huo, Xiangtao
Gao, Jianming
Cheng, Fangqin
Zhang, Mei
coredata.dc:description: The intricate composition and structural of low-rank coal have hindered its broad utilization in the field of material science. The study utilized an alkaline hydrothermal method to enhance the depolymerization of long-flame coal and achieve the purification of fluorescent carbon dots for the first time, which were then being employed for the trace detection of Cu2+ or Fe3+ ions. The carbon extraction efficiency reached 58 %, with the carbon dots averaging size of 1.36 ± 0.42 nm, the sp2 hybridized structure of the carbon dots indicated successfully purification under alkaline conditions. After reduction with sodium borohydride, defects in the core region of the carbon dots were repaired, and the C = O functional groups were transformed into O–H groups, leading to an increase in fluorescence intensity. At a concentration of 0.25 g/L of carbon dots, demonstrated high emission intensity at both long and low emission wavelengths, which can be used as LED luminescent material. Furthermore, the application of these carbon dots in detecting heavy metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+) in aqueous solutions was explored, showing as low as detection limit of ∼ 0.04 μM. This study achieved higher depolymerization of coal and purification of fluorescent carbon dots for use in probe-based detection, providing fresh perspectives on the high-value utilization of low-rank coal.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long-flame coal
Enhanced oxidative depolymerization
Alkaline conditions
Fluorescent carbon dots
Carbon dots purification
Ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction efficiency optimizing of coal oxidation
Capacity of carbon dots for detecting trace Cu2+ and Fe3+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U21A20321) are duly acknowledged for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of the alkali/coal ratio on the extraction efficiency of long flame coal.

Characterizing the morphology and structure of the synthesized nanoparticles (a) Low-magnification image of carbon nanoparticles, the size distributions shown in the inset; (b) High-resolution TEM micrographs providing detailed views of the carbon nanoparticles; (c) Selected area electron diffraction ring corresponding to the nanoparticles; (d) EDS spectrum capturing elemental composition information of the nanoparticles.
(a) XRD pattern of carbon dots; (b) Raman spectra of carbon dots; (c) FT-IR spectra of raw coal and carbon dots.
Fluorescence spectra of carbon dots hydrosol at different concentrations, inset shows the digital photo of the sample under UV light (365 nm).

(a) Relationship between Stokes shift and excitation wavelength of 0.25 g/L sample; (b) PL spectra of carbon dots with different concentrations at 400 nm excitation wavelength, inset table showed the relationship between concentration and maximum emission wavelength; (c) PL spectra of carbon dots with different concentrations at 480 nm excitation wavelength, inset table showed the relationship between concentration and maximum emission wavelength; (d) The relationship between concentration and fluorescence intensity under different excitation wavelength.
Basic schematic diagram of a simple carbon dots structure and its corresponding fluorescence emission mechanism.
(a) Fluorescence intensity of carbon dots (0.25 g/L) before and after the addition of NaBH4; (b) FT-IR of carbon dots before and treated with NaBH4 after dehydration.

(a) The dependence of F/F0 on the concentration of Cu2+, inset showed the corresponding fluorescence response of the carbon dots in Cu2+ solutions with various concentrations; (b)Stern-Volmer plot for the fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots by Cu2+ in the low concentrations of 0 ∼ 0.5 μM, inset showed the digital photographs of carbon dot hydrogels under a UV lamp with different concentrations of Cu2+.

(a) The dependence of F/F0 on the concentration of Fe3+, inset showed the corresponding fluorescence response of the carbon dots in Fe3+ solutions with various concentrations of Fe3+; (b)Stern-Volmer plot for the fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots by Fe3+ in the low concentrations of 0 ∼ 0.5Μm, inset showed the digital photographs of carbon dots hydrogels under a UV lamp with different concentrations of Fe3+.

PL delay (black line) and fitting (red line) curves of (a)carbon dots without ions; (b)carbon dots with 200 μM Cu2+; (c) carbon dots with 200 μM Fe3+. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Mild Alkaline-Enhanced depolymerization of Long-Flame coal for the synthesis of Coal-Derived fluorescent carbon dots with application as probes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Metallurgy, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Metallurgy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Metallurgy, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgical and Ecological Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Metallurgical and Ecological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Metallurgical and Ecological Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Engineering Research Center of the Ministry of Education for CO2 emission reduction and resource utilization, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Resources and Environmental Engineering
Engineering Research Center of the Ministry of Education for CO2 emission reduction and resource utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Engineering Research Center of the Ministry of Education for CO2 emission reduction and resource utilization, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The intricate composition and structural of low-rank coal have hindered its broad utilization in the field of material science. The study utilized an alkaline hydrothermal method to enhance the depolymerization of long-flame coal and achieve the purification of fluorescent carbon dots for the first time, which were then being employed for the trace detection of Cu2+ or Fe3+ ions. The carbon extraction efficiency reached 58 %, with the carbon dots averaging size of 1.36 ± 0.42 nm, the sp2 hybridized structure of the carbon dots indicated successfully purification under alkaline conditions. After reduction with sodium borohydride, defects in the core region of the carbon dots were repaired, and the C = O functional groups were transformed into O–H groups, leading to an increase in fluorescence intensity. At a concentration of 0.25 g/L of carbon dots, demonstrated high emission intensity at both long and low emission wavelengths, which can be used as LED luminescent material. Furthermore, the application of these carbon dots in detecting heavy metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+) in aqueous solutions was explored, showing as low as detection limit of ∼ 0.04 μM. This study achieved higher depolymerization of coal and purification of fluorescent carbon dots for use in probe-based detection, providing fresh perspectives on the high-value utilization of low-rank coal.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Constituting around 55 % of China's overall coal resources, low-rank coal possesses ample reserves and presents lower costs, rendering it highly suitable for extensive application and commercialization. However, due to the limitations of existing technology, low-rank coal was primarily used for combustion power generation, leading not only to the waste of resources but also causing severe environmental pollution[1]. Hence, the most logical strategy for achieving the clean and efficient utilization of low-rank coal lies in transforming it into diverse high-value-added carbon materials, aligning with its structural characteristics. The structure of coal is characterized by the intricate embedding of sp2 and sp3 carbon domains. Over the past decade, more than 100 models have been proposed for various types of coals, thereby expanding the possibilities for carbon material preparation[1]. Generally, higher-rank coals exhibit a structural similarity to graphite with fewer impurities and a more straightforward materialization process, due to their increased proportion of sp2 structures, enhanced stability, and reduced reactivity during chemical reactions, they were often utilized as graphite-like structures, such as activated carbon[2], conductive carbon black[3] and other materials[4]. Low-rank coal exhibits a more intricate composition with embedded distribution of sp2 and sp3 bonds, lower degree of coalification, reduced calorific value, elevated moisture content, and higher volatility. However, low-rank coal has heightened levels of hydrogen and oxygen content, coupling with increased reactivity and well-established primary pores, proving the advantageous for oxidation reactions[5].

The employment of oxidation processes in treating low-rank coal has been a prominent research area within coal utilization studies. This approach not only enhanced the reactive properties of low-rank coal, thereby increasing its energy efficiency, but also facilitated the selective production of targeted compounds through the fine-tuning of reaction parameters, consequently enabling the synthesis of high-value products with added commercial significance[6,7]. Miura presented a methodology for oxidizing lignite under mild conditions, achieving the high yields and selectivity for small-molecule fatty acids, including malonic acid, glycolic acid, formic acid, and acetic acid. The oxidation mechanism proposed involves the breaking of the weaker −C−O− bonds in lignite, leading to the formation of water-soluble macromolecules and CO2, these macromolecules undergo further oxidation and degradation, resulting in the small-molecule fatty acids. Concurrently, the cleavage of certain aromatic rings also contributes to the generation of these acids[8,9]. Beyond the isolation of small molecular compounds, strategically dismantling weak bonds while preserving stable crystalline carbon domains in coal offers another significant avenue for high-value coal utilization. Hu synthesized carbon nanodots employing H2O2 coupled with ultrasonication, demonstrating remarkable fluorescence characteristics[10]. Das processed low-rank coal with an ultrasonic-assisted wet chemical method to make carbon dots for Ag+ ion detection, which exhibited great potential for utilization in high-end application like sensing of hazardous elements[11]. In our preliminary work, low-rank coal was successfully oxidized using hydrogen peroxide, resulting in the preparation of carbon dots through a straightforward heating method, tunable fluorescence was accomplished by varying the concentration of these carbon dots throughout the procedure[12].

The addition of alkali was beneficial for the depolymerization of coal, Lu's density functional theory calculations revealed that alkali supplementation could decrease the reaction energy of polycyclic groups, thereby enhancing side chain/bridge bond oxidation and instigating ring-opening reactions[13]. Wang found that coal could be more fully oxidized by oxygen/air in alkaline aqueous solution, and then obtain a more satisfactory yield of benzene polycarboxylic acid[14]. Concurrently, Bai introduced alkali during the oxidation procedure to modulate carbon dots morphology, marking significant progress in solar energy hydrogen production[15]. The feasibility of enhancing the depolymerization of low-rank coals and efficiently producing carbon nanoparticles by integrating alkali into the oxidation process presents an intriguing query. Additionally, studies have shown that under hydrothermal conditions, the oxygen-containing functional groups in coal are initially destroyed, while the stable graphitic structures within the coal are sufficiently preserved, leading to an enhancement in the quality of the coal[16,17].

Fe and Cu are essential elements for the maintenance of vital functions in most animals and plants and are also crucial trace elements in the human body. However, excessive levels of Fe3+ and Cu2+ can promote to produce the reactive oxygen species, leading to various neurodegenerative disorders, such as Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease[18]. Additionally, an accumulation of excessive Cu2+ or Fe3+ in the human body can cause damage to the liver and kidneys. Historically, methods for detecting Fe3+ or Cu2+ in water primarily included atomic absorption spectrophotometry[19], colorimetry[20], electrochemical analysis[21], and ion chromatography[22]. Nevertheless, these techniques typically required precise equipment, complicated preprocessing steps, and relatively high testing costs. Fluorescence techniques, known for their high sensitivity, easy handling, and clear visualization, were widely used for detecting metal ions in recent years[23]. Consequently, the development of a low-cost, simple-to-operate method for ion detection was particularly important.

In this study, based on the structural analysis and mild oxidative depolymerization of Shanxi low-rank coal, the hydrothermal oxidative depolymerization of long-flame coal under alkaline conditions were further experimentally investigated. We elucidated the reaction mechanism, optimized the experimental parameters to achieve maximum coal utilization efficiency, and successfully purified carbon nanoparticles using an alkaline medium. Additionally, the photoluminescent performance of carbon nanoparticles prepared was evaluated. It's noteworthy that it was the first study to efficiently prepare and purify nano-carbon particles using low-rank coal as the base material through a simple alkaline medium method, and to utilize them for Fe3+ or Cu2+ probe detection. This study provides a novel approach for the oxidative depolymerization of low-rank coal, as well as the synthesis and purification of carbon nanoparticles, and enabled trace visualization detection of heavy metal ions, offering a new insight for the high-value utilization of low-rank coal.

Long-flame coal, was obtained from the northern region of Shanxi province, China, and subjected to water washing, vacuum drying at 80 °C for 24 h, and subsequently crushed to a particle size below 200 mesh. The industrial analysis and ultimate analyses of the coal used in the experiment have been reported in previous literature[1]. The depolymerisation reaction of coal involved sodium hydroxide (Sinopharm, China), polytetrafluoroethylene membrane and hydrogen peroxide are consistent with those reported in previous literature[1].

To investigate the differences in oxidative depolymerization of coal under different alkali-coal ratio conditions, the specific experimental design as follows: 0.20 g sample of washed long flame coal was placed in a hydrothermal reactor with different masses of sodium hydroxide (0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40, 0.50, 0.60 g), the corresponding alkali-coal ratio was 0.5; 1.0; 1.5; 2.0; 2.5; 3.0, followed by the addition of 60 mL of 20 wt% H2O2 solution, and the reaction was carried out for 9 h at 80 °C in a homogeneous reactor. After the completion of the reaction, the hydrosol was filtered and collected. The calculation of the extraction efficiency and the methodology employed were consistent with those reported in the literature[12].

Following oxidation reaction, the hydrosol underwent straightforward dialysis and subsequent freeze-drying, yielding solid particles (carbon dots). A 1.50 g/L hydrosol of carbon dots was prepared using deionized water, which was subsequently diluted to various concentrations (0.10 ∼ 1.00 g/L) for photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and essential structural characterization. Afterwards, added 0.10 g NaBH4 to 20 mL of carbon dots hydrosol and stirred for 12 h to generate reduced carbon dots.

CuCl2 and FeCl3 were used to prepare aqueous solutions of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions at various concentrations, respectively. Subsequently, carbon dots were added to each of these samples. The fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots hydrosol with different concentrations of metal ions were determined. F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities of the hydrosol at the maximum excitation wavelength in blank carbon dots samples and presence various concentrations of Fe3+or Cu2+ ions, respectively. This was utilized to evaluate the sensitivity of carbon dots at various concentrations of Fe3+or Cu2+.
The equipment used is that described in previous work[12]. Additionally, fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a FLS1000 time-corrected single-photon counting system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction efficiency optimizing of coal oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation of long-flame coal was investigated under varying alkali/coal ratios to determine the extraction efficiency of the reaction. Fig. 1 illustrated the carbon extraction efficiency at different alkali-coal ratios, revealing an initial increase followed by a gradual decrease with increasing alkali-coal ratio, the extraction rate was 37.56 % without any alkali addition, and the coal extraction rate increased to 58.54 % when the alkali-coal ratio was 1.5, and as the alkali-coal ratio continued to increase, the extraction rate showed a decreasing trend.

In previous studies, the highest extraction efficiency of carbon under condensation reflux conditions without the addition of alkali was 30.86 %[12]. In this experiment, under hydrothermal reaction conditions the extraction efficiency increased to 37.56 %. The improvement may be attributed to the following reasons: Within hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radicals were recognized as highly potent oxidizing agents, these radicals possessed the ability to selectively cleave vulnerable bonds within coal, transforming unstable carbon into either organic compounds or carbon dioxide (CO2)[12]. In experiments conducted within a reaction vessel, the high-temperature and high-pressure conditions provided more favorable thermodynamic and kinetic conditions for the generation of hydroxyl radicals from hydrogen peroxide. Additional, the high-temperature and high-pressure conditions also directly caused large carbon chain molecules in coal to undergo condensation reactions, this process led to the absorption of energy by unstable groups within the coal molecular clusters, resulting in the breaking and removal of functional groups and the formation of compounds such as organic acids[24]. Consequently, this rendered extraction efficiency by the hydrothermal method was superior to that of the condensation reflux method.

In the presence of an alkaline environment (with an alkali-to-coal ratio of less than 1.5), the extraction efficiency shows an upward trend. The reason for this was that under alkali solution conditions, the interlayer spacing of the aromatic layers in coal increases, leading to a decrease in stacking density, aromatic nucleation extensibility, and the number of aromatic layers. This resulted in a more loosened structure, which was more conducive to the hydrolysis of coal[25]. Moreover, under alkaline conditions, coal underwent deep oxidation. The resulting carboxylic acids stabilized as carboxylate salts, preventing excessive oxidation and CO2 release[26], thereby improving the extraction efficiency. When the alkaline content was excessively high during the oxidation process (alkali/coal ratio＞1.5), the enhanced salting-out effect due to the increased alkaline concentration delayed the diffusion of intermediate products into the aqueous phase, the parent structure of benzene polycarboxylic acids generated from coal oxidation may happen excessive oxidation to carbon dioxide, resulting in a decrease in extraction efficiency[14].

In hydrothermal experiments conducted under different alkali/coal ratios, we observed that a ratio of 1.5 yielded the maximum extraction efficiency at 58.54 %, achieving optimal carbon conversion for low-rank coal. Subsequently, through a simple filtration process, carbon dots particles in the hydrosol were efficiently extracted, enabling their independent and effective utilization.

The filtrate contained not only small molecules but also a substantial amount of high-value-added carbon nanoparticles. The 24 h dialysis of the filtrate was conducted to remove small molecules, the hydrosol within the dialysis bag went freeze-drying to carry out the subsequent characterization step.

Fig. 2 (a) showed the morphology of carbon nanoparticles by transmission electron microscope, and the inset showed the statistics of the particle size distribution of carbon spots in the field of view (A Gaussian fit performed using Image J software yielded a statistical count of over 500 carbon nanoparticles, with an R-squared value of 97.51 %.), after statistical calculations it could be concluded that the size of the prepared carbon nanoparticles was uniform and was 1.36 ± 0.42 nm, which was significantly lower than that of without alkali of 1.86 ± 0.62 nm, indicating that the edges of the carbon dots were decomposed, in other words the carbon dots were purified[12]. Fig. 2b showed the high-resolution spectrum of carbon nanoparticles, the lattice stripes were clearly visible, and their structures were mostly single-crystal structures with lattice spacings of 0.210 nm, which were close to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, and there were about 8 ∼ 10 stacked monatomic graphitic layers as shown in Fig. 2b, consequently, the carbon nanoparticles can be categorized as graphitic carbon dots. Fig. 2c displayed the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of the carbon dots, revealing diffused rings that signified the polycrystalline nature of these dots[27]. However, the incomplete crystallization process could be ascribed to the diminutive size of the carbon dots, aligning with trends observed in other literature[28]. Fig. 2d demonstrated the analysis of the chemical composition through energy-dispersive spectroscopy. The results indicated that the particles predominantly comprise carbon, with traces of Si attributed to impurities of the coal. Additionally, Cu elements were detected, stemming from the copper TEM grid. Energy spectrum also revealed the presence of O elements in these nanoparticles, possibly originating from surface-adsorbed oxidation groups. Excluding the influence of Cu element, the proportion of O element in carbon dots (2.95 %) was lower than that without adding alkali (3.80 %)[12], depicting that the number of oxygen-containing functional groups in carbon dots was significantly reduced under alkaline conditions.

Fig. 3 a illustrated the XRD spectrum of the nanoparticles, revealing (002) peaks around 26° indicative of an interlayer spacing approximately 0.34 nm. This closely aligned with graphite (≈0.35 nm), providing strong evidence that the core regions of carbon dots predominantly consist of densely packed, highly conjugate sp2 carbon domains[29]. The Raman of carbon dots was illustrated in Fig. 3b, in which the G band could be attributed to the in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon, reflecting the formation of graphitic carbon, and the D band ascribed to the defects and imperfections. In addition, where the disordered D band at about 1372 cm−1 was slightly weaker than the ordered G band at about 1582 cm−1 with the D/G ratio of 0.83, obviously lower than raw coal D/G ratio of 1.00, indicating that the stable graphite structure dominates in the structure of carbon dots[30].

In previous studies, it has been confirmed that raw coal contained abundant aliphatic and aromatic structures, with bridging bonds such as –CH2− and –CH2−O− existing between the aromatic layers[1]. In the direct oxidation preparation of carbon dots using hydrogen peroxide, the C = O content was significantly higher than that of C = C, with the presence of functional groups such as C = O, C−H, C−O and O–H[12]. The FT-IR of raw coal and carbon dots prepared after alkali treatment was shown in Fig. 3c, after oxidative depolymerization by alkali treatment, the stretching vibration of the aromatic structure (C = C) in the carbon dots was attributed to the high-intensity peaks observed at approximately 1600 cm−1 and 1440 cm−1. These peaks originated from the remaining polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon region within the carbon dots[30]. The observed reduction or disappearance of the characteristic peaks corresponding to the bridging bonds between aromatic layers and aliphatic structures in carbon dots, compared to raw coal, indicated a transformation in these specific structural components of the coal. In the oxidation process, the aliphatic chains within raw coal exhibited high reactivity, making them susceptible to facile breakage by hydroxyl free radicals[10,12,30]. Therefore, through treatment by hydrogen peroxide with alkali solution, the carbon dots exhibited their most prominent peak at approximately 1440 cm−1, accompanied by a shoulder peak around 1600 cm−1. This observation indicated the oxidative cleavage of aliphatic chains and the subsequent formation of oxygen-bearing functional groups, such as −C = O and −COOH. Notably, the persistence of the 1600 cm−1 peak in the carbon dots' spectrum underscored the retention of their polycyclic aromatic core structure[29]. The relative content of C = C surpassed that of C = O, O–H, or other functional groups, indicating that the carbon dots predominantly featured a stable graphite structure, the oxygen-containing functional groups were disrupted, demonstrating that the fundamental structure of carbon dots prepared under alkaline conditions was purified.

Based on the above, highly crystalline graphitic carbon dot samples were successfully prepared and purified, exhibiting an average particle size of 1.36 ± 0.42 nm, ready for subsequent experimentation.

The phenomenon of fluorescence was one of the most interesting properties of carbon dots. Fig. 4 depicted the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of carbon dots at different concentrations, derived from solid carbon dots obtained through dialysis and freeze-drying, it could be clearly seen that the distinct fluorescent behavior exhibited by the hydrogel at varying concentrations. The inset displayed the digital photograph of the hydrogel when exposed to UV light (365 nm), it can be observed that with the increase in carbon dots concentration, the color of the corresponding hydrogel gradually shifted from blue to lighter shades. Additionally, we have supplemented the CIE spectra under different excitation wavelengths, as shown in Supporting Information (Figure S1). It can be observed that with increasing carbon dot concentration, there is a redshift in the emission wavelength, a phenomenon extensively reported in previous literature[12]. From Fig. 4, it also can be seen that when the carbon dots concentration was 0.10 g/L, the maximum emission wavelength was approximately ∼ 440 nm (marked in blue color), while at a concentration of 1.50 g/L, the maximum emission wavelength shifted to around ∼ 535 nm (marked in orange color). The fluorescence behavior of carbon dots varied with changes in concentration and was closely associated with alterations in their structure. In response to changes in carbon dots concentration, distinctions in fluorescence behavior were observed at emission peaks around ∼ 440 nm and ∼ 535 nm, which were designated as the low-emission wavelength center and long-emission wavelength center, respectively[31].

It can be inferred from Fig. 4 that with an increase in carbon dots concentration, the fluorescence intensity at the low emission fluorescence center continuously decreased, while at the long emission fluorescence center, it initially increased and then decreased. This difference implied that the radiative recombination processes of electron-hole pairs might have originated from diverse types of electron excited states within coal-based carbon dots. When the carbon dots concentration was 0.25 g/L, strong intensity excitation was observed at both wavelengths around 440 nm and 535 nm. Additionally, compared with other carbon dots based on plant or chemical reagents, the emission spectrum exhibited significant breadth[32,33], suggesting a strong potential for the preparation of full-spectrum fluorescence materials, such as those utilized in LED manufacturing[31,34].

Fig. 5 a illustrated the Stokes shift curve for the sample with a concentration of 0.25 g/L. The Stokes shift, signifying the discrepancy in the peak positions between the excitation and emission spectra, generally indicated that the main energy level structure or electronic states remained unchanged as it linearly decreased. In other words, variations in the linear trend of the Stokes shift of a sample could depict the emergence of new electronic states or changes of the existing electronic states[35]. In our case, the Stokes shift of carbon nanoparticles initially linearly decreased, then underwent a modification, continuing to linearly decrease in a new manner. The two linear trends corresponded to the two fluorescence center, indicating the presence of two distinct fluorescence mechanisms[36]. Fig. 5b and Fig. 5c depicted emission spectra of carbon dots at varying concentrations under same excitation wavelengths of ∼ 400 nm and ∼ 480 nm, corresponding to their low and long emission fluorescence centers, respectively.

The concentration significantly influenced the fluorescence behavior, primarily because the increase in concentration led to the aggregation of carbon dots, this aggregation and the resulting particle size effect induced changes in the energy band structure of the carbon dots. In our experiment, as shown in Fig. 5b and Fig. 5c, while carbon dots concentration increased, there was a redshift of approximately 50 nm in the emission wavelength under 400 nm excitation. In contrast, for the long wave excitation (480 nm), the fluorescence peak remained stable around 535 nm, showing minimal sensitivity to concentration changes. This suggested that the low excitation fluorescence centers may be somewhat correlated with the particle size effect, long excitation fluorescence centers may be related to defects or functional groups of carbon dots[31]. Fig. 5d illustrated the relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of carbon dots hydrogel at varying excitation wavelengths, it was observed that under low excitation wavelengths (280 nm or 300 nm, corresponded to black or red lines), the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots gradually decreased as their concentration increased, this reduction in fluorescence intensity could be attributed to the aggregation of carbon dots particles at higher concentrations. Under higher excitation wavelengths (480 nm or 500 nm, corresponded to blue or green lines), the fluorescence intensity initially increased and then decreased. This trend could be explained by the increase in the number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups as the concentration of carbon dots rose, providing additional electronic states or affecting the surface states, thereby enhancing the fluorescence initially. However, the excessive introduction of functional groups likely resulted in non-radiative recombination centers and altered the electronic structure of the carbon dots, leading to a decrease in fluorescence efficiency. Similar phenomena have been reported in other literature[12,31].

The overall fluorescence phenomenon of carbon dots can be explained by the emission of low emission wavelengths and long wavelengths, the basic structure of carbon dots and the corresponding luminescent mechanism are illustrated in Fig. 6 . The blue region represented clusters with low emission wavelength fluorescence, corresponding to the emission from the core state of the sp2 structural domain. Within this structural domain, π electrons formed a conjugated structure, and the quantum size effect promoted π → π transitions, resulting in the emission of higher-energy light, generating intrinsic state fluorescence, namely blue fluorescence (∼440 nm). The orange region represented long emission wavelength fluorescence clusters, corresponding to the fluorescence generated by the interaction of various surface defects induced by oxygen atoms in carbon dots and functional groups effects. These defects were similar to organic fluorophores, and the radiation recombination of electron-hole pairs at these defect sites led to photo-induced fluorescence, namely green fluorescence (∼535 nm)[5,37].

The structural mechanism also can be used to explain the fluorescence phenomenon of samples with different concentrations (Fig. 5d). When under low excitation wavelengths (280 nm or 300 nm) conditions and the concentration of carbon dots was low (0.10 g/L), they were well-dispersed within the hydrosol, the fluorescence characteristics were predominantly governed by the quantum size effects of the carbon dots, when the concentration of carbon dots increased, the quantum size effect weakened due to the aggregation of carbon dots, leading to an expansion in the motion range of electrons and holes, thus the band gap decreased. This manifested as a sustained decrease in fluorescence intensity under low excitation wavelength conditions, which was similar to that reported in the literature for small-sized carbon dots[23,31]. Under long excitation wavelengths (480 nm or 500 nm), the fluorescence intensity exhibited an initial increase followed by a decrease trend. This was primarily attributed to the enhancement of fluorescence with the increase in functional groups content as the concentration rose. However, when the concentration exceeded 0.25 g/L, an excess of functional groups heightened the likelihood of non-radiative recombination, leading to a reduction in fluorescence intensity[31].

Sodium borohydride was a mild reducing agent that could be employed to elucidate the luminescent mechanism of carbon dots and enhance fluorescence intensity[29,37]. To verify the carbon dots structure and luminescence mechanism, photoluminescence (PL) tests were conducted on carbon dots reduced with NaBH4, and the results were provided in the Supporting Information (Figure S2). Taking a 0.25 g/L carbon dots sample as an example, Fig. 7 a illustrated the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots before and after the addition of NaBH4. It was observed that the overall fluorescence exhibited an enhancement trend, with the fluorescence intensity of the low-emission center increasing most significantly, under the excitation wavelength conditions of 360 nm, the fluorescence intensity increased by 117 %, and under the excitation wavelength conditions of 480 nm, it increased by 28 %. The impact of NaBH4 on the structure of carbon dots can be described as follows: As a moderately strong reducing agent, the addition of sodium borohydride initially repaired surface defects on the carbon dots, leading to the formation of numerous sp2 microdomains. The reduction by NaBH4 could led to a slight increase in the size of carbon dots, manifested as an enhancement in fluorescence intensity and a redshift in the maximum emission wavelength (where the shift greater than 0 represented a redshift)[23,37], as indicated by the lower excitation wavelengths shown in Supporting Information (Table S1). FT-IR spectroscopy was used to characterize the carbon dots’ structure before and after the reaction, the strong polarity of water results in a prominent infrared absorption peak that often obscured other absorption signals, as depicted in Supporting Information (Figure S3). Therefore, the detection of dehydration became essential in such analyses. The FT-IR of the carbon dots before and after the sodium borohydride reaction following dehydration were illustrated in Fig. 7b, C = C and O–H were the predominant structures in carbon dots. In comparison with carbon dots untreated, it was indicated that NaBH4 reacted with the C = O functional groups in aldehydes and ketones, converting them into alcohols (O–H), the peaks corresponding to the C = O bonds at 882 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 was weaken due to reduction[30], while the C = C bond at 1602 cm−1 showed no significant change[31], the introduction of sodium borohydride led to the appearance of B-H bonds within the system, which was consistent with other nanoparticle literature report[38]. The emission wavelength of the O–H groups was lower than that of C = O, and as electron-donating groups, they aided in enhancing fluorescence[35]. This also explained the observed blue shift under long excitation conditions (a shift less than 0 in Table S1 indicating a blue shift) and the associated increase in fluorescence intensity[5,29].

In summary, coal-based carbon dots exhibited strong photoluminescence. When the concentration of hydrosol was 0.25 g/L, a broad emission spectrum was observed. The fluorescence mechanism was primarily attributed to the combined effects of low fluorescence emission centers caused by quantum size effects induced by sp2 domains and long fluorescence emission centers resulting from functional groups emissions. After the addition of NaBH4, the quantum size effects in the carbon dots and the formation of O–H functional groups led to the enhancement of fluorescence intensity at both the low and long emission centers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capacity of carbon dots for detecting trace Cu2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescent carbon dots exhibited stable fluorescence properties, chemical inertness, non-toxicity, and environmental friendliness, making them a potential low-cost fluorescent probe for ion detection in environmental monitoring and bioanalysis. In the present work, the potential of carbon dots as probes for Cu2+and Fe3+ metal ions were detailed investigated by measuring their fluorescence changes in aqueous solutions containing Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions. The results demonstrated that the F/F0 ratio was depended on the concentration of Cu2+and Fe3+, as separately illustrated in Fig. 8 (a) and Fig. 9 (a). A discernible decline in the F/F0 ratio was noted with increasing concentrations of Cu2+and Fe3+, the F/F0 versus Cu2+and Fe3+ concentration curve plateaued at higher concentrations (>200 µM), for which the reason was the chelating sites on the carbon dots for Cu2+and Fe3+binding nearing saturation[23]. In the fields of photochemistry and fluorescence spectroscopy, the Stern-Volmer equation stands as a pivotal relationship, as shown in equation (2), it articulated the decay of fluorescence when a quenching agent was present.
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of the probes in the absence and presence of the concerned ions solution. The term KSV denotes the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] refers to the concentration of the quenching agent, μM.

The Stern-Volmer plot for Cu2+ and Fe3+ induced fluorescence quenching showed a linear relationship within the concentration range of 0 to 0.5 µM, as illustrated in Fig. 8(b) and Fig. 9(b), respectively. The detection limit, which was established following the definition provided by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC)[23], when the signal-to-noise ratio was 3, the limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+and Fe3+ was determined to be 0.038 and 0.034 μM (The detailed calculation and the fluorescence intensity of the blank signal were shown in Supporting Information in Equation S1 and Table S2, respectively). This indicated that carbon dots, when utilized for detecting Cu2+ or Fe3+ ions in the solution, demonstrated superior performance compared to most fluorescent probes, detailed comparisons can be found in the Supporting Information (Table S3), suggesting promising prospects for practical applications. The inset in Fig. 8b and Fig. 9b showed digital photographs of carbon dots hydrogel exposuring to ultraviolet light with varying concentrations of Cu2+ and Fe3+. It was observed that as the concentration of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions increased, the fluorescence of the carbon dots gradually diminished, eventually leading to the absence of fluorescence. This method possessed advantages such as intuitive visual results, simple operation, and sensitive detection, and could be further developed for the preparation of colorimetric fluorescence probes.

The introduction of metal ions resulted in a reduction in fluorescence, a phenomenon commonly referred to as fluorescence quenching, and this property enabled carbon dots to serve as a fluorescent probe. The reduction in fluorescence intensity typically arises from static quenching and/or dynamic quenching mechanisms, static quenching involves the formation of complexes when ground-state fluorescent molecules combine with quencher molecules, while dynamic quenching involves collisions between excited fluorescent molecules and quencher molecules[39,40]. The fluorescence lifetime of carbon dots serves as a valuable technique for distinguishing between static and dynamic quenching processes. This is accomplished by comparing the fluorescence lifetimes in the absence and presence of quencher molecules. If the lifetimes remain nearly unchanged, it indicates static quenching. Conversely, if there is a significant difference between the two lifetimes, it suggests dynamic quenching[41].

Fig. 10 illustrated the results obtained from fluorescence lifetime spectroscopy, and the detailed fitting data were shown in Supporting Information (Table S4). The photoluminescence decay curves were fitted to a double-exponential function, the average decay time at the carbon dots was 4.92 ns. In the presence of carbon dots with Cu2+, the average decay time was 4.89 ns, and in the presence of carbon with Fe3+, the average decay time was 4.95 ns. The nearly identical values confirmed that the photoluminescence quenching of carbon dots in the presence of Cu2+ or Fe3+ were caused by static quenching.

In this paper, an alkaline hydrothermal method was first employed to process long-flame coal, facilitating depolymerization of the coal and purification of fluorescent carbon dots. When the alkali/coal ratio was set at 1.5, the extraction efficiency of coal reached 58.54 %, and the carbon dots produced had an average size of 1.36 ± 0.42 nm. NaBH4 reduction repaired the defects in the core region of the carbon dots and induced the transformation of C = O into O–H functional groups. This led to a significant overall enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of both low and long fluorescence centers. When the concentration of the carbon dots was 0.25 g/L, exhibited high emission intensity at both long and low excitation wavelength, demonstrating their immense potential for the fabrication of full-spectrum fluorescence materials, such as in LED manufacturing. Moreover, in the lower concentration range of Fe3+ or Cu2+ (0 ∼ 0.5 μM), a linear correlation was observed between the fluorescence signal of the carbon dots and the presence of heavy metal ions in aqueous solutions, the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ and Cu2+ was determined to be 0.034 or 0.038 μM, respectively. These results indicated that fluorescent carbon dots have great promise for the detection of Fe3+ or Cu2+. Hence, this study provided new insights into the efficient conversion of low-rank coal and purification of carbon dots from low-rank coal, and achieved trace, visual, and simple detection of heavy metal ions.

Junkai Chong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Guangchao Hu: Data curation. Longheng Xiao: Data curation. Min Guo: Methodology. Xiangtao Huo: Investigation. Jianming Gao: Supervision. Fangqin Cheng: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Mei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
